news_summary: Today’s space news highlights breakthroughs in planetary defense, innovative methods for detecting extraterrestrial life, and the discovery of a massive cosmic structure surrounding the Milky Way.
news_summary_fr: Les actualités spatiales d'aujourd'hui mettent en avant les avancées en matière de défense planétaire, les méthodes innovantes de détection de la vie extraterrestre et la découverte d'une structure cosmique massive entourant la Voie lactée.

articles.title: NASA changed an asteroid's orbit around the sun for the first time
articles.summary: NASA’s DART mission slammed into the small asteroid Dimorphos in 2022, and the impact slowed its orbit around the larger Didymos – and also the pair’s path around the sun
articles.content:
NASA’s DART mission slammed into the small asteroid Dimorphos in 2022, and the impact slowed its orbit around the larger Didymos – and also the pair’s path around the sun

NASA gave the Didymos system a nudge Steve Gribben/Johns Hopkins APL/NASA

Humanity has shifted an asteroid’s orbit around the sun for the first time. This was achieved by NASA’s Double Asteroid Redirection Test (DART) mission in 2022, but the effect has only now been measured.

DART’s target was a small asteroid called Dimorphos, which orbits a larger one called Didymos. The spacecraft crashed into the smaller rock in an effort to shift its orbit around the larger one, testing whether this method, called a kinetic impactor, would be an effective way to change an asteroid’s trajectory if it were heading towards Earth and send it careening past safely.

The mission was a smashing success, shortening the length of Dimorphos’s orbit by 32 minutes. In the years since then, astronomers have continued to watch the system, and with nearly 6000 observations, they have been able to calculate the change in the pair’s overall orbit around the sun: it has slowed down by 11.7 micrometres per second, or around 40 millimetres per hour. That is expected to decrease the radius of its orbit by about 360 metres.

“It doesn’t sound like a lot, but the whole idea behind these kinetic impacts is that if you do one early enough, a small impact makes a large change in the overall position,” says Rahil Makadia at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, part of the team monitoring the asteroids’ orbits. “It’s a very tiny number, but if you let it accumulate over decades, then it can grow into a big one.”

The slowdown had two causes: the initial impact by the spacecraft and an additional push from the jet of debris sent up from Dimorphos’s surface in the aftermath. Makadia and his colleagues calculated that the two effects were about equal to one another, which in turn allowed them to calculate the masses and densities of the asteroids. Dimorphos is about half as dense as Didymos, which adds credence to the idea that it is a so-called rubble pile that formed from material flung off of Didymos due to its rotation.

All of this information will prove useful should we ever need to deflect a hazardous asteroid for real. “We now have one solid anchoring point to predict any future kinetic impact missions,” says Makadia. And the European Space Agency’s Hera spacecraft, which is on its way towards Didymos and is expected to arrive in November, should give us even more precise measurements that will guide any future attempts to protect Earth from incoming asteroids.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and successful space mission where humanity altered an asteroid's orbit for the first time, demonstrating a viable method to protect Earth from potential asteroid impacts. The achievement has broad significance for planetary defense and future space missions, with detailed context and measurable impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA changes an asteroid’s path around the sun for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 2022, NASA launched a mission called DART (Double Asteroid Redirection Test) to test if hitting an asteroid with a spacecraft could change its path. This method, called a kinetic impactor, aims to protect Earth from possible asteroid collisions.

**What happened** – DART crashed into a small asteroid named Dimorphos, which orbits a larger asteroid called Didymos. The impact shortened Dimorphos’s orbit around Didymos by 32 minutes. Scientists have now measured that this collision also slowed down the pair’s orbit around the sun by a tiny amount.

**Impact** – Although the change in speed—about 11.7 micrometres per second—is very small, it will slowly shift the asteroids’ path over many years. This proves that even a small push can make a big difference if done early enough. This success helps us understand how to protect Earth from dangerous asteroids.

**What’s next step** – The European Space Agency is sending a spacecraft named Hera to study Didymos and Dimorphos more closely. Hera will arrive in November and provide more detailed data to improve future asteroid deflection missions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s DART mission proved that hitting an asteroid can change its orbit around the sun, showing a real way to protect Earth from asteroid threats.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA modifie pour la première fois la trajectoire d’un astéroïde autour du soleil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 2022, la NASA a lancé la mission DART (Double Asteroid Redirection Test) pour tester si percuter un astéroïde avec un engin spatial pouvait changer sa trajectoire. Cette méthode, appelée impact cinétique, vise à protéger la Terre des collisions possibles avec des astéroïdes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – DART s’est écrasé sur un petit astéroïde nommé Dimorphos, qui orbite autour d’un plus grand astéroïde appelé Didymos. L’impact a raccourci l’orbite de Dimorphos autour de Didymos de 32 minutes. Les scientifiques ont maintenant mesuré que cette collision a aussi ralenti légèrement la trajectoire du couple autour du soleil.

**Impact** – Bien que le changement de vitesse – environ 11,7 micromètres par seconde – soit très faible, il déplacera lentement la trajectoire des astéroïdes sur plusieurs années. Cela prouve qu’une petite poussée peut avoir un grand effet si elle est faite suffisamment tôt. Ce succès aide à comprendre comment protéger la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – L’Agence spatiale européenne envoie un engin spatial nommé Hera pour étudier Didymos et Dimorphos de plus près. Hera arrivera en novembre et fournira des données plus précises pour améliorer les futures missions de déviation d’astéroïdes.

**Conclusion** – La mission DART de la NASA a démontré qu’il est possible de modifier l’orbite d’un astéroïde autour du soleil, offrant une méthode concrète pour protéger la Terre des menaces d’astéroïdes.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA cambia por primera vez la órbita de un asteroide alrededor del sol
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 2022, la NASA lanzó la misión DART (Double Asteroid Redirection Test) para probar si chocar una nave espacial contra un asteroide podía cambiar su trayectoria. Este método, llamado impacto cinético, busca proteger la Tierra de posibles colisiones con asteroides.

**Qué pasó** – DART chocó contra un pequeño asteroide llamado Dimorphos, que orbita un asteroide más grande llamado Didymos. El impacto acortó la órbita de Dimorphos alrededor de Didymos en 32 minutos. Ahora, los científicos midieron que esta colisión también desaceleró levemente la órbita del par alrededor del sol.

**Impacto** – Aunque el cambio de velocidad—unos 11,7 micrómetros por segundo—es muy pequeño, con el tiempo moverá lentamente la trayectoria de los asteroides. Esto demuestra que un pequeño empujón puede hacer una gran diferencia si se hace con suficiente anticipación. Este éxito ayuda a entender cómo proteger la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – La Agencia Espacial Europea enviará una nave llamada Hera para estudiar Didymos y Dimorphos con más detalle. Hera llegará en noviembre y dará datos más precisos para mejorar futuras misiones de desviación de asteroides.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La misión DART de la NASA demostró que chocar un asteroide puede cambiar su órbita alrededor del sol, mostrando una forma real de proteger la Tierra de amenazas de asteroides.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two rocky asteroids orbiting closely in space, with a small, glowing spacecraft gently colliding with the smaller asteroid, creating a subtle burst of dust and debris that forms a delicate arc around them, symbolizing the successful nudging of their shared orbit around the sun.

articles.title: Chemistry clues could detect aliens unlike any life on Earth
articles.summary: Looking for molecular evidence of life on other worlds is tricky, but a test based on the reactivity of carbon compounds could be a useful indicator
articles.content:
Looking for molecular evidence of life on other worlds is tricky, but a test based on the reactivity of carbon compounds could be a useful indicator

Enceladus, a moon of Saturn, is a prime target in the hunt for life elsewhere in our solar system NASA/JPL/Space Science Institute

A new method to recognise the chemical properties of living things could help us detect alien life even if it functions differently from life on Earth.

When searching for alien life, scientists usually rely on biosignatures – substances or patterns that can reliably indicate the presence of living organisms. Astronomers can analyse the atmospheres of faraway planets to look for molecular biosignatures. But many molecules produced by living things can also arise through geological or chemical processes in the absence of life forms.

The new test, devised by Christopher Carr at the Georgia Institute of Technology and his colleagues, is based on amino acids. Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins, complex molecules that all life on Earth depends on. However, amino acids are relatively simple molecules, and they can occur in the absence of life: for example, they have been found in lunar soil and on comets and meteorites.

So, rather than simply detecting amino acids, Carr and his colleagues reasoned that measuring the reactivity of the molecules in a sample would be a more reliable indicator of living things.

In a non-living system, molecules are formed and destroyed as they react with things in their environment, like cosmic rays or other molecules, but the more reactive molecules are more likely to disappear. “If you don’t have a system in place to maintain what’s present, then the things that will tend to be destroyed would be those that are more reactive,” says Carr. Living systems, however, will preferentially keep more reactive molecules because they require them for the chemical processes that support life, leading to a unique signature.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

The reactivity of a compound is determined by the arrangement of electrons in the molecule. More reactive molecules have a smaller difference in energy between the outermost electron and the next available space that would be filled by an additional electron during a reaction.

Carr and his team calculated this difference in energy for 64 amino acids, including many that aren’t used by life on Earth. Then they looked up amino acid abundances in known samples, which came from either abiotic sources, like meteorites or moon soil, or from living samples, like fungi or bacteria, and used their molecular energy calculations to map the statistical distribution of amino acid reactivities. From this, they could then assign a probability that the sample was living or non-living.

Using this method on more than 200 living and non-living samples, they found it could identify life correctly 95 per cent of the time. “The beauty of this approach is that it’s incredibly simple,” says Carr. “It’s highly explainable and it’s linked directly to physics.”

Life, if it does exist elsewhere in the universe, is likely to be based on carbon chemistry and amino acids, and function according to the same chemical reactivity rules as life on Earth, argues Carr, so this method should work for extraterrestrial life, he says. “Life inherently needs to control when, how and where molecules interact and reactions take place, so that is going to involve having structures that can regulate the flow of electrons and how things interact electrically,” says Carr.

Using the reactivity of molecules to detect life isn’t a new idea, but measuring the reactivity in a statistical distribution is, says Henderson Cleaves at Howard University in Washington DC. The method could form part of a suite of life-detecting tools on a future space mission to Mars or one of Saturn’s moons, like Enceladus, but it would require equipment that can accurately measure molecules and their abundances, which isn’t straightforward, says Cleaves.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel scientific method to detect alien life by analyzing molecular reactivity, which represents a meaningful breakthrough in astrobiology. This method could significantly enhance the search for extraterrestrial life, a topic with broad public interest and potential large-scale impact on our understanding of life in the universe. The article provides sufficient detail on the scientific approach and its implications, focusing on a single, significant advancement.
articles.personality_title: New chemical test could help find alien life unlike anything on Earth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists search for signs of life on other planets by looking for molecules called biosignatures. These are chemicals that living things produce. But many of these molecules can also form without life, making it hard to tell if they really mean life is there.

**What happened** – A team led by Christopher Carr at the Georgia Institute of Technology created a new way to spot life by studying how reactive certain molecules are. They focused on amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins and important for life on Earth. Instead of just finding amino acids, they measured how these molecules react with their environment. Living things keep more reactive molecules because they need them to survive, while non-living places lose these molecules faster.

**Impact** – This new method can tell if a sample comes from living or non-living sources with 95% accuracy. It works by looking at the energy in the molecules and how they react, which is linked to basic physics. The test could find life even if it is very different from Earth life, as long as it follows the same chemical rules. This is important because it gives scientists a better tool to detect alien life on moons like Enceladus or planets like Mars.

**What's next step** – To use this method on space missions, scientists need equipment that can measure molecules and their amounts very precisely. If this is developed, the test could be part of future missions looking for life beyond Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new test that measures how molecules react could help scientists find alien life, even if it is very different from life on Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau test chimique pourrait aider à détecter une vie extraterrestre différente de celle sur Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent des signes de vie sur d'autres planètes en recherchant des molécules appelées biosignatures. Ce sont des substances produites par des êtres vivants. Mais beaucoup de ces molécules peuvent aussi se former sans vie, ce qui rend difficile de savoir si elles indiquent vraiment la présence de la vie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Christopher Carr à l'Institut de Technologie de Géorgie a créé une nouvelle méthode pour détecter la vie en étudiant la réactivité de certaines molécules. Ils se sont concentrés sur les acides aminés, qui sont les éléments de base des protéines et essentiels à la vie sur Terre. Plutôt que de simplement chercher les acides aminés, ils ont mesuré la façon dont ces molécules réagissent avec leur environnement. Les êtres vivants conservent les molécules les plus réactives parce qu'ils en ont besoin pour survivre, tandis que dans un milieu non vivant, ces molécules disparaissent plus vite.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode peut déterminer si un échantillon vient d'une source vivante ou non vivante avec une précision de 95 %. Elle fonctionne en observant l'énergie dans les molécules et leur réactivité, ce qui est lié à la physique fondamentale. Le test pourrait détecter la vie même si elle est très différente de celle sur Terre, tant qu'elle suit les mêmes règles chimiques. C’est important car cela donne aux scientifiques un meilleur outil pour détecter la vie extraterrestre sur des lunes comme Encelade ou des planètes comme Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – Pour utiliser cette méthode lors de missions spatiales, les scientifiques doivent développer des instruments capables de mesurer les molécules et leurs quantités avec une grande précision. Si cela est réalisé, le test pourrait faire partie des futures missions à la recherche de vie au-delà de la Terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau test mesurant la réactivité des molécules pourrait aider les scientifiques à trouver une vie extraterrestre, même si elle est très différente de la vie terrestre.
articles.personality_title_es: Un nuevo test químico podría ayudar a encontrar vida alienígena diferente a la de la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan señales de vida en otros planetas buscando moléculas llamadas biosignaturas. Son sustancias producidas por seres vivos. Pero muchas de estas moléculas también pueden formarse sin vida, lo que dificulta saber si realmente indican la presencia de vida.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo dirigido por Christopher Carr en el Instituto de Tecnología de Georgia creó una nueva manera de detectar vida estudiando qué tan reactivas son ciertas moléculas. Se centraron en los aminoácidos, que son los componentes básicos de las proteínas y esenciales para la vida en la Tierra. En lugar de solo encontrar aminoácidos, midieron cómo estas moléculas reaccionan con su entorno. Los seres vivos mantienen las moléculas más reactivas porque las necesitan para sobrevivir, mientras que en sistemas sin vida estas moléculas desaparecen más rápido.

**Impacto** – Este método puede decir si una muestra proviene de fuentes vivas o no vivas con un 95% de precisión. Funciona observando la energía en las moléculas y cómo reaccionan, lo que está relacionado con las leyes básicas de la física. La prueba podría encontrar vida incluso si es muy diferente a la de la Tierra, siempre que siga las mismas reglas químicas. Esto es importante porque ofrece una mejor herramienta para detectar vida alienígena en lunas como Encélado o planetas como Marte.

**Próximo paso** – Para usar este método en misiones espaciales, los científicos necesitan desarrollar equipos que puedan medir con precisión las moléculas y sus cantidades. Si se logra, esta prueba podría formar parte de futuras misiones en busca de vida fuera de la Tierra.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo test que mide la reactividad de las moléculas podría ayudar a los científicos a encontrar vida alienígena, incluso si es muy diferente a la vida en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing cluster of diverse amino acid molecules interconnected by subtle electric currents, floating above a softly illuminated icy landscape symbolizing Enceladus, with gentle cosmic rays and distant stars in the background highlighting the search for alien life through chemical reactivity.

articles.title: Astronomers discover giant cosmic sheet around the Milky Way
articles.summary: For decades, astronomers wondered why most nearby galaxies are speeding away from the Milky Way instead of being pulled in by its gravity. New simulations reveal the answer: our galaxy sits in a gigantic, flat sheet of matter surrounded by huge empty voids. This hidden structure—dominated by dark matter—balances gravitational forces and lets neighboring galaxies drift outward. The discovery finally explains the puzzling motions of galaxies just beyond our Local Group.
articles.content:
Nearly a century ago, astronomer Edwin Hubble discovered that almost all galaxies are receding from the Milky Way. This observation became a cornerstone of modern cosmology because it provided key evidence that the universe is expanding and that it began with the Big Bang. Even during Hubble's era, however, astronomers knew the pattern was not universal. One notable exception is our neighboring galaxy Andromeda, which is moving toward the Milky Way at roughly 100 kilometers per second.

For about fifty years, scientists have puzzled over another related mystery. Most large galaxies near our own, aside from Andromeda, appear to be moving away from us rather than being pulled inward by gravity. This seems surprising because these galaxies reside near the Local Group (the Milky Way, the Andromeda Galaxy and dozens of smaller galaxies), whose combined mass should exert a noticeable gravitational influence.

A Giant Cosmic Sheet Around the Local Group

An international research team led by PhD graduate Ewoud Wempe of the Kapteyn Institute in Groningen believes it has found the explanation. Using advanced computer simulations, the researchers discovered that the matter surrounding the Local Group is arranged in a broad, flattened structure that stretches tens of millions of light-years across. This structure includes not only ordinary matter but also the invisible dark matter that surrounds galaxies. Above and below this flattened region lie enormous empty areas known as cosmic voids.

The simulations show that this arrangement of matter can accurately reproduce both the positions and speeds of the galaxies observed around us. In other words, the computer model successfully recreates the same patterns astronomers see in the real universe.

Creating a Virtual Twin of Our Cosmic Neighborhood

To build their model, the scientists began with conditions from the early universe. They used measurements of the cosmic microwave background to estimate how matter was distributed shortly after the Big Bang. A powerful computer then evolved this early universe forward in time, eventually producing a system that matches the present day Local Group.

The resulting simulations replicate the masses, locations, and motions of the Milky Way and Andromeda, as well as the positions and velocities of 31 galaxies just outside the Local Group. Because the model so closely resembles our surroundings, researchers describe it as a "virtual twin" of our cosmic environment.

When the model includes the flat distribution of matter, the surrounding galaxies move away from us at speeds similar to those actually observed. Despite the gravitational pull of the Local Group, galaxies within the plane are influenced by additional mass spread throughout that same plane. This distant mass counterbalances the Local Group's gravity. Meanwhile, regions outside the plane contain very few galaxies, which explains why we do not see objects falling toward us from those directions.

A Longstanding Puzzle Finally Explained

According to lead researcher Ewoud Wempe, the study represents the first detailed attempt to determine the distribution and motion of dark matter in the area around the Milky Way and Andromeda. "We are exploring all possible local configurations of the early universe that ultimately could lead to the Local Group. It is great that we now have a model that is consistent with the current cosmological model on the one hand, and with the dynamics of our local environment on the other."

Astronomer Amina Helmi also welcomed the findings, noting that the problem has challenged researchers for decades. "I am excited to see that, based purely on the motions of galaxies, we can determine a mass distribution that corresponds to the positions of galaxies within and just outside the Local Group."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough explaining a longstanding cosmic mystery about galaxy motions near the Milky Way. The discovery of a giant cosmic sheet dominated by dark matter that balances gravitational forces has broad significance for understanding our local universe and cosmology. The detailed simulations and modeling provide substantial context and represent a meaningful advance in astrophysics.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find giant flat cosmic sheet around the Milky Way explains galaxy movements
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, astronomers have noticed that most nearby galaxies are moving away from the Milky Way, even though gravity should pull them closer. The only big exception was the Andromeda galaxy, which moves toward us. This puzzling pattern did not fit well with what scientists expected about gravity and galaxy movements.

**What happened** – A team of researchers led by Ewoud Wempe from the Kapteyn Institute used powerful computer simulations to study the area around the Milky Way and its nearby galaxies. They discovered that the Milky Way sits inside a huge, flat sheet of matter that stretches tens of millions of light-years. This sheet contains both normal matter and invisible dark matter. Around this sheet are large empty spaces called cosmic voids. Their model, which started from conditions just after the Big Bang, closely matches the real positions and speeds of galaxies near us.

**Impact** – This discovery explains why galaxies near the Milky Way, except Andromeda, move away instead of falling inward. The flat sheet of matter spreads out the gravitational pull, balancing forces and allowing galaxies to drift outward. It solves a question that scientists have struggled with for about fifty years and helps us better understand the structure of our cosmic neighborhood.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to refine these simulations to learn more about how dark matter shapes our local universe. They may also use similar models to study other galaxy groups and improve our knowledge of how the universe evolved after the Big Bang.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A giant, flat sheet of normal and dark matter around the Milky Way explains why nearby galaxies mostly move away from us, solving a decades-old cosmic mystery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une immense feuille cosmique plate autour de la Voie lactée qui explique le mouvement des galaxies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les astronomes ont remarqué que la plupart des galaxies proches s’éloignent de la Voie lactée, même si la gravité devrait les attirer. La seule exception majeure était la galaxie d’Andromède, qui se dirige vers nous. Ce comportement étrange ne correspondait pas aux attentes sur la gravité et le mouvement des galaxies.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Ewoud Wempe de l’Institut Kapteyn a utilisé des simulations informatiques puissantes pour étudier la région autour de la Voie lactée et ses galaxies proches. Ils ont découvert que la Voie lactée se trouve dans une immense feuille plate de matière qui s’étend sur des dizaines de millions d’années-lumière. Cette feuille contient de la matière ordinaire et de la matière noire invisible. Autour de cette feuille, il y a de grands espaces vides appelés vides cosmiques. Leur modèle, basé sur les conditions juste après le Big Bang, correspond bien aux positions et vitesses réelles des galaxies proches.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi les galaxies proches de la Voie lactée, sauf Andromède, s’éloignent au lieu de tomber vers nous. La feuille plate de matière répartit la force gravitationnelle, équilibrant les forces et permettant aux galaxies de s’éloigner. Cela résout une question que les scientifiques cherchaient à comprendre depuis environ cinquante ans et améliore notre compréhension de la structure de notre voisinage cosmique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer ces simulations pour mieux comprendre comment la matière noire façonne notre univers local. Ils pourraient aussi utiliser des modèles similaires pour étudier d’autres groupes de galaxies et approfondir nos connaissances sur l’évolution de l’univers après le Big Bang.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une immense feuille plate de matière ordinaire et de matière noire autour de la Voie lactée explique pourquoi les galaxies proches s’éloignent en majorité, résolvant un mystère cosmique vieux de plusieurs décennies.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran una enorme lámina cósmica plana alrededor de la Vía Láctea que explica el movimiento de las galaxias
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los astrónomos han observado que la mayoría de las galaxias cercanas se alejan de la Vía Láctea, aunque la gravedad debería atraerlas. La única excepción importante era la galaxia de Andrómeda, que se mueve hacia nosotros. Este patrón desconcertante no encajaba con lo que se esperaba sobre la gravedad y el movimiento de las galaxias.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores liderado por Ewoud Wempe del Instituto Kapteyn usó potentes simulaciones por computadora para estudiar la zona alrededor de la Vía Láctea y sus galaxias vecinas. Descubrieron que la Vía Láctea se encuentra dentro de una enorme lámina plana de materia que se extiende por decenas de millones de años luz. Esta lámina contiene materia normal y materia oscura invisible. Alrededor de esta lámina hay grandes espacios vacíos llamados vacíos cósmicos. Su modelo, basado en las condiciones justo después del Big Bang, coincide bien con las posiciones y velocidades reales de las galaxias cercanas.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento explica por qué las galaxias cercanas a la Vía Láctea, excepto Andrómeda, se alejan en lugar de acercarse. La lámina plana de materia distribuye la fuerza gravitacional, equilibrando las fuerzas y permitiendo que las galaxias se alejen. Esto resuelve una cuestión que los científicos han intentado entender durante unos cincuenta años y mejora nuestra comprensión de la estructura de nuestro vecindario cósmico.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán perfeccionando estas simulaciones para aprender más sobre cómo la materia oscura da forma a nuestro universo local. También podrían usar modelos similares para estudiar otros grupos de galaxias y mejorar nuestro conocimiento sobre la evolución del universo después del Big Bang.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una enorme lámina plana de materia normal y oscura alrededor de la Vía Láctea explica por qué la mayoría de las galaxias cercanas se alejan de nosotros, resolviendo un misterio cósmico de décadas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast, gently curved cosmic sheet glowing softly in shades of deep blue and muted gold, stretching across a starry sky, with stylized swirling clusters representing the Milky Way and Andromeda galaxies nestled within the sheet, surrounded by expansive dark voids that emphasize the flat, expansive structure of matter and dark matter shaping their motions.
